lists.title: German A1 Grammar Path
lists.description: A1 grammar in communicative order: pronouns, the three genders, both article families, sein and haben, the present tense, verb-second word order, negation with nicht and kein, es gibt, questions, and the first case (accusative).

points.title: Subject pronouns (ich, du, er…)
points.function: Say who you are talking about
points.explanation: The subject pronouns are ich (I), du (you, informal), er/sie/es (he/she/it), wir (we), ihr (you all, informal), sie (they) and Sie (you, formal — always capitalized). Formal Sie takes the same verb form as 'they'.
points.culture_note: Germans switch from Sie to du more slowly than you might expect — wait for the offer ('Wir können uns duzen').

points.title: Gender and number of nouns
points.function: Name people and things, one or many
points.explanation:
German nouns have THREE genders — masculine (der Mann), feminine (die Frau), neuter (das Kind) — and are always capitalized.

Plurals form several ways (-e, -er, -en, -s, umlaut: Buch → Bücher, Haus → Häuser), so learn each noun with its gender AND its plural.

drills.translation: The children are playing in the garden.
I have two cats at home.
The tables are made of wood.

points.title: Definite articles (der / die / das)
points.function: Point to a specific thing
points.explanation: Der for masculine (der Mann), die for feminine (die Frau), das for neuter (das Haus) — and die again for every plural (die Kinder). The article is the gender's uniform: memorize noun and article as one word.

points.title: Indefinite articles (ein / eine)
points.function: Mention something for the first time
points.explanation:
Ein for masculine and neuter (ein Mann, ein Haus), eine for feminine (eine Frau). There is no plural indefinite article — plural just drops it: Das sind Bücher.

The endings change with case; that story starts with the accusative later in this path.

drills.translation: He is a friend of mine.

points.title: Sein — present (ich bin, du bist…)
points.function: Say who or what someone is
points.explanation:
Sein (to be) is completely irregular: ich bin, du bist, er/sie/es ist, wir sind, ihr seid, sie/Sie sind.

Professions and nationalities take no article: sie ist Lehrerin, ich bin Deutscher.

drills.translation: The children are in the garden.

points.title: Haben — present (ich habe Hunger)
points.function: Say what you have — and how you feel
points.explanation:
Haben (to have): ich habe, du hast, er/sie hat, wir haben, ihr habt, sie/Sie haben.

Like French, German HAS its states: Hunger haben (be hungry), Durst haben (be thirsty), Recht haben (be right), Glück haben (be lucky).

drills.translation: You (all) have a lot of work.

points.title: Present tense of regular verbs
points.function: Talk about what people do
points.explanation:
Drop -en from the infinitive, add: -e, -st, -t, -en, -t, -en (lernen → ich lerne, du lernst, er lernt, wir lernen, ihr lernt, sie lernen).

German present covers English 'do' and 'am doing' both: ich lerne = I learn AND I am learning.

drills.translation: My sister sings in a choir.
The children are playing in the park.

points.title: Verb second (V2) word order
points.function: Build German sentences in the German order
points.explanation:
The conjugated verb is ALWAYS the second element of a statement.

Start with anything else — time, place — and the subject slides behind the verb: Ich lerne heute Deutsch → Heute lerne ich Deutsch.

The verb stays put; everything else dances around it.
points.culture_note: V2 is the single most German thing about German — internalize it at A1 and everything later gets easier.

drills.hint: to learn, I — verb stays second

drills.translation: Tomorrow we are travelling to Berlin.
drills.hint: to travel, we — verb stays second
to play, he — verb stays second
to drink, she — verb stays second

drills.translation: In the evenings I listen to music.
drills.hint: to listen, I — verb stays second
to live, she — verb stays second

points.title: Negation with nicht and kein
points.function: Say what is not so
points.explanation:
Two negators with a clean split: kein negates nouns with ein or no article (ich habe kein Auto, keine Zeit); nicht negates everything else — verbs, adjectives, definite nouns (das ist nicht richtig, ich wohne nicht hier).

Kein agrees like ein: kein/keine.

points.title: Es gibt — there is / there are
points.function: Say what exists or is available
points.explanation:
Es gibt covers 'there is' AND 'there are' — always singular gibt: es gibt einen Park, es gibt viele Cafés. What exists goes in the accusative (einen Park).

Negative: es gibt kein/keine…

drills.sentence: Es {{answer}} einen Park in der Nähe.
drills.hint: the verb of the fixed phrase
the verb of the fixed phrase

points.title: Question words (wo, wie, was…)
points.function: Ask about places, people, time, and reasons
points.explanation: Wo (where), wie (how), was (what), wann (when), warum (why), wer (who), woher (where from), wohin (where to). The question word takes first position and the verb follows — V2 again: Wo wohnst du?

drills.translation: Where are you going on holiday?

points.title: Accusative case (den / einen)
points.function: Mark the direct object
points.explanation:
The direct object takes the accusative — and only MASCULINE articles change: der → den, ein → einen (ich sehe den Mann, ich habe einen Hund).

Feminine, neuter, and plural look identical to the nominative: ich lese das Buch, ich sehe die Frau.
points.culture_note: One changing gender is a gentle introduction — dative and genitive wait politely at A2.

points.title: The perfect with haben
points.function: Talk about the past in speech
points.explanation:
Spoken German uses the perfect: a present form of haben + the past participle at the END of the clause.

The participle is usually ge-…-t (gemacht, gespielt) or ge-…-en (gesehen, gelesen): ich habe Fußball gespielt. haben is conjugated (habe, hast, hat, haben, habt, haben); the participle never changes.

drills.sentence: Heute Morgen {{answer}} ich einen Kaffee getrunken.
drills.translation: This morning I drank a coffee.
When did you see this film?
At the weekend we danced a lot.
Why did you (pl.) wait so long?
The children played in the garden.

drills.sentence: Meine Freunde {{answer}} das ganze Wochenende gearbeitet.
drills.translation: My friends worked the whole weekend.

points.title: The perfect with sein
points.function: Use sein for movement and change of state
points.explanation: Verbs of movement or change of state (gehen, kommen, fahren, fliegen, laufen, bleiben, werden, sterben…) form the perfect with sein, not haben: ich bin nach Berlin gefahren 'I went to Berlin'. sein is conjugated (bin, bist, ist, sind, seid, sind); the participle still goes to the end.

drills.translation: Yesterday I went to the cinema.

drills.sentence: Am Sonntag {{answer}} wir in die Stadt gefahren.
drills.translation: On Sunday we went into town.

points.title: The dative case (dem, der, den…)
points.function: Mark the indirect object — 'to/for whom'
points.explanation:
The dative marks the indirect object (the receiver).

The articles change: der→dem, die→der, das→dem, and plural die→den (+ -n on the noun): ich gebe dem Mann das Buch 'I give the man the book'.

Dative verbs (helfen, danken, gefallen) and prepositions (mit, nach, bei, von, zu, aus) also trigger it.

drills.translation: I give the man the book.

drills.sentence: Sie hilft {{answer}} Frau mit den Taschen.
drills.translation: She helps the woman with the bags.
The child likes the gift a lot.

drills.sentence: Ich fahre mit {{answer}} Bus zur Arbeit.
drills.translation: I go to work by bus.

drills.sentence: Wir danken {{answer}} Lehrern für ihre Hilfe.
drills.translation: We thank the teachers for their help.
After the meal we drink coffee.

points.title: Modal verbs (können, müssen, wollen…)
points.function: Say what you can, must, or want to do
points.explanation: Modal verbs (können 'can', müssen 'must', wollen 'want', dürfen 'may', sollen 'should', mögen/möchte 'like/would like') are conjugated in position 2, and the main verb goes to the END as an infinitive: ich kann gut schwimmen, du musst jetzt gehen. Note the vowel change: ich kann, wir können.

drills.sentence: Wir {{answer}} am Wochenende ins Kino gehen.
drills.translation: We want to go to the cinema at the weekend.
I would like a coffee, please.

points.title: Simple past of sein & haben (war, hatte)
points.function: Say 'was' and 'had' in the past
points.explanation:
Even in speech, sein and haben usually use the simple past (Präteritum) rather than the perfect. sein: war, warst, war, waren, wart, waren. haben: hatte, hattest, hatte, hatten, hattet, hatten.

Ich war müde 'I was tired', wir hatten keine Zeit 'we had no time'.

drills.translation: Were you at the party yesterday?
We were in Spain last year.
As a child I had a dog.
We had a lot of fun on holiday.

points.title: Two-way prepositions (in, auf, an…)
points.function: Choose accusative for motion, dative for location
points.explanation: Nine prepositions (in, an, auf, über, unter, vor, hinter, neben, zwischen) take the ACCUSATIVE for movement toward a place (wohin?) and the DATIVE for a fixed location (wo?): ich gehe in die Küche (acc., motion) vs ich bin in der Küche (dat., location).

drills.translation: Hang the picture on the wall! (motion)
The picture is hanging on the wall. (location)
The cat jumps onto the table. (motion)
The cat is sleeping on the table. (location)

points.title: Separable verbs (aufstehen, einkaufen)
points.function: Split the prefix off to the end of the clause
points.explanation:
Many verbs have a separable prefix (auf-, ein-, an-, mit-, zurück-…) that splits off and goes to the END in a main clause: aufstehen → ich stehe um sieben auf 'I get up at seven'; einkaufen → wir kaufen im Supermarkt ein.

The prefix carries the stress and often changes the meaning.

drills.sentence: Ich stehe jeden Tag um sieben Uhr {{answer}}.
drills.translation: I get up at seven every day.

drills.sentence: Der Zug kommt um zehn Uhr {{answer}}.
Wann kommst du aus dem Urlaub {{answer}}?
drills.translation: When are you coming back from holiday?

points.title: Comparative & superlative (größer als, am größten)
points.function: Compare things
points.explanation:
The comparative adds -er and uses als 'than' (often with an umlaut): groß → größer als 'bigger than'.

The superlative is am …-sten: am größten 'the biggest'.

Irregulars: gut → besser → am besten, viel → mehr → am meisten, gern → lieber → am liebsten.

drills.sentence: Dieses Café ist am {{answer}} in der Stadt.
drills.translation: This café is the best in town.

drills.sentence: Deutsch ist {{answer}} als Spanisch für mich.
drills.translation: German is harder than Spanish for me.
In summer the days are longer.
He plays football better than me.
Health is more important than money.

points.title: Possessive articles (mein, dein, sein…)
points.function: Say 'my / your' and match case & gender
points.explanation:
Possessives (mein, dein, sein, ihr, unser, euer, ihr/Ihr) take the same endings as ein/kein: mein Vater (nom. m.), meine Mutter (nom. f.), meinen Bruder (acc. m.), meinem Freund (dat. m.).

The stem shows the owner; the ending agrees with the noun.

drills.translation: I'm visiting my brother at the weekend.

points.title: Subordinate clauses (weil, dass)
points.function: Give reasons and report — verb goes to the end
points.explanation: Subordinating conjunctions (weil 'because', dass 'that', wenn 'if/when', obwohl 'although') send the conjugated verb to the END of their clause: ich bleibe zu Hause, weil ich krank bin (bin at the end). Compare coordinating denn 'because', which keeps normal order.

drills.sentence: Ich bleibe zu Hause, {{answer}} ich krank bin.
drills.translation: I'm staying home because I'm ill.
I think that he is right.

drills.sentence: Wir gehen spazieren, {{answer}} es schön ist.
drills.translation: We go for a walk when the weather is nice.

drills.sentence: {{answer}} es regnet, gehen wir ins Museum.
drills.translation: Because it's raining, we're going to the museum.
She says that she'll come later.

drills.sentence: Ich lerne Deutsch, {{answer}} es mir gefällt.
drills.translation: I'm learning German because I like it.

points.title: The genitive case (des, der)
points.function: Show possession — 'of the'
points.explanation:
The genitive marks possession ('of'). Masculine and neuter articles become des and the noun adds -(e)s: das Auto des Mannes 'the man's car', das Dach des Hauses.

Feminine and plural become der (no noun ending): die Farbe der Blume.

Genitive prepositions: wegen, während, trotz, statt.

drills.translation: The colour of the flower is red.
The roof of the house is old.

drills.sentence: Wegen {{answer}} Wetters bleiben wir zu Hause.
drills.translation: Because of the weather we're staying home.
The children's toys are lying there.

drills.sentence: Während {{answer}} Reise haben wir viel gesehen.
drills.translation: During the trip we saw a lot.

points.title: The simple past (Präteritum)
points.function: Narrate the past in writing
points.explanation:
Written German (stories, reports) uses the Präteritum.

Weak verbs add -te: machen → machte, spielen → spielte.

Strong verbs change the stem: gehen → ging, kommen → kam, sehen → sah, fahren → fuhr.

Endings: -,-st,-, -en,-t,-en (ich ging, du gingst, wir gingen).

drills.translation: I did my homework in the evening.

drills.sentence: Wir {{answer}} mit dem Zug nach Berlin.
drills.translation: We went to Berlin by train.

drills.sentence: Plötzlich {{answer}} ein Fremder an die Tür.
drills.translation: Suddenly a stranger knocked at the door.

points.title: Adjective endings
points.function: Give adjectives before a noun the right ending
points.explanation:
An adjective before a noun takes an ending. After a definite article (der/die/das) it's mostly -e or -en (der alte Mann, die alten Männer).

After an indefinite/possessive (ein/mein) it shows the gender: ein alter Mann, eine alte Frau, ein altes Haus.

With no article, the adjective carries the full article ending (kalter Kaffee).

drills.sentence: Der {{answer}} Mann sitzt auf der Bank.
drills.translation: The old man is sitting on the bench.
We live in a big house.

points.title: Reflexive verbs (sich freuen)
points.function: Say the subject acts on itself
points.explanation:
Reflexive verbs use a reflexive pronoun matching the subject: mich, dich, sich, uns, euch, sich (accusative).

Ich freue mich 'I'm happy', er erinnert sich 'he remembers'. Some are dative-reflexive when there's also a direct object: ich wasche mir die Hände 'I wash my hands'.

drills.translation: I'm looking forward to the weekend.
We're meeting tomorrow at the café.
Hurry up (pl.), the bus is coming!

points.title: Relative clauses (der, die, das, dem…)
points.function: Describe a noun with a whole clause
points.explanation: The relative pronoun matches the noun's GENDER and NUMBER but takes its CASE from its role in the relative clause, which is verb-final: der Mann, der dort steht (nom.); der Mann, den ich kenne (acc.); der Mann, dem ich helfe (dat.); der Mann, dessen Auto rot ist (gen.).

drills.sentence: Der Mann, {{answer}} dort steht, ist mein Lehrer.
drills.translation: The man who is standing there is my teacher.

drills.sentence: Das Buch, {{answer}} ich lese, ist spannend.
drills.translation: The book that I'm reading is exciting.

drills.sentence: Die Frau, {{answer}} ich helfe, ist krank.
drills.translation: The woman I'm helping is ill.

drills.sentence: Der Freund, {{answer}} ich ein Geschenk gebe, hat Geburtstag.
drills.translation: The friend I'm giving a present to has a birthday.

drills.sentence: Die Kinder, {{answer}} draußen spielen, sind laut.
drills.translation: The children who are playing outside are loud.

drills.sentence: Das Auto, {{answer}} er gekauft hat, ist rot.
drills.translation: The car that he bought is red.

points.title: The Konjunktiv II (würde, wäre, hätte)
points.function: Be polite and talk about hypotheticals
points.explanation:
Konjunktiv II expresses politeness, wishes, and unreal situations. Most verbs use würde + infinitive: ich würde gern kommen 'I would like to come'.

But sein, haben, and the modals use their own forms: wäre, hätte, könnte, müsste, wollte.

Ich hätte gern einen Kaffee 'I'd like a coffee'.

drills.sentence: An deiner Stelle {{answer}} ich das nicht tun.
drills.translation: In your place I wouldn't do that.
It would be nice if you came.
Could you listen to me for a moment?
We would like to have more time.

points.title: Infinitive with zu (um … zu)
points.function: Link two verbs, and express purpose
points.explanation:
After many verbs and expressions, a second verb takes zu + infinitive at the end: ich versuche zu schlafen 'I try to sleep', es ist wichtig, pünktlich zu sein.

To express purpose ('in order to'), use um … zu: ich lerne, um die Prüfung zu bestehen. Separable verbs insert zu: aufzustehen.

drills.translation: It's important to be on time.

drills.sentence: Ich lerne, {{answer}} die Prüfung zu bestehen.
drills.translation: I study in order to pass the exam.

drills.sentence: Er ging in die Stadt, {{answer}} einzukaufen.
drills.translation: He went into town in order to shop.

drills.sentence: Hast du Lust, ins Kino {{answer}} gehen?
drills.translation: Do you feel like going to the cinema?

drills.sentence: Es ist nicht leicht, eine Sprache {{answer}} lernen.
drills.translation: It's not easy to learn a language.

points.title: The passive voice (werden + Partizip II)
points.function: Say an action is done, without the doer
points.explanation:
The passive = werden (conjugated) + the past participle at the end: das Haus wird gebaut 'the house is being built'.

Any tense works via werden: wurde gebaut (past), ist gebaut worden (perfect — note worden, not geworden).

The agent, if named, follows von: … von einem Architekten.

drills.translation: The house is being built right now.

drills.sentence: Das Museum {{answer}} von vielen Touristen besucht.
drills.translation: The museum is visited by many tourists.
The cake has already been eaten.
This decision will be made tomorrow.

drills.sentence: Das Bild wurde {{answer}} einem berühmten Maler gemalt.
drills.translation: The painting was painted by a famous painter.

points.title: The past perfect (Plusquamperfekt)
points.function: Set one past event before another
points.explanation: The Plusquamperfekt ('had done') = the Präteritum of haben/sein + the past participle: ich hatte gegessen 'I had eaten', sie war gegangen 'she had left'. It marks an action finished before another past point, often with nachdem 'after': nachdem ich gegessen hatte, ging ich.

drills.sentence: Als du ankamst, {{answer}} ich schon gegessen.
drills.translation: When you arrived, I had already eaten.

drills.sentence: Der Zug {{answer}} schon abgefahren, als wir ankamen.
drills.translation: The train had already left when we arrived.

drills.sentence: Nachdem sie gegangen {{answer}}, wurde es still.
drills.translation: After she had left, it became quiet.

drills.sentence: Wir {{answer}} das Essen schon bestellt, als er kam.
drills.translation: We had already ordered the food when he came.

drills.sentence: Ich erkannte das Haus nicht; es {{answer}} sich verändert.
drills.translation: I didn't recognize the house; it had changed.

drills.sentence: Nachdem er die Prüfung bestanden {{answer}}, feierte er.
drills.translation: After he had passed the exam, he celebrated.

points.title: Unreal conditions (Konjunktiv II der Vergangenheit)
points.function: Say what would have happened
points.explanation: For unreal PAST conditions, use hätte/wäre + past participle in both clauses: wenn ich Zeit gehabt hätte, wäre ich gekommen 'if I had had time, I would have come'. wenn introduces the condition (verb-final); the result uses hätte/wäre + participle, or würde + infinitive for the present.

drills.sentence: Wenn ich Zeit gehabt {{answer}}, wäre ich gekommen.
drills.translation: If I had had time, I would have come.

drills.sentence: Wenn du früher gegangen {{answer}}, hättest du den Zug erreicht.
drills.translation: If you had left earlier, you'd have caught the train.

drills.sentence: Ich {{answer}} dir geholfen, wenn du gefragt hättest.
drills.translation: I would have helped you if you had asked.
We would have liked to stay longer.

drills.sentence: Wenn es nicht geregnet {{answer}}, hätten wir gegrillt.
drills.translation: If it hadn't rained, we would have had a barbecue.

drills.sentence: Was {{answer}} du an meiner Stelle gemacht?
drills.translation: What would you have done in my place?

points.title: Connectors & word order (deshalb, trotzdem, damit)
points.function: Link ideas and get the verb position right
points.explanation:
Connectors set word order. Coordinating (und, aber, oder, denn) keep normal order.

Adverbial ones (deshalb 'therefore', trotzdem 'nevertheless', dann) push the subject after the verb: es regnet, deshalb bleiben wir. Subordinating (weil, obwohl, damit, wenn) send the verb to the END.

drills.sentence: Es regnet, {{answer}} bleiben wir zu Hause.
drills.translation: It's raining, so we're staying home.

drills.sentence: Er ist müde; {{answer}} arbeitet er weiter.
drills.translation: He's tired; nevertheless he keeps working.

drills.sentence: Ich spreche laut, {{answer}} alle mich hören.
drills.translation: I speak loudly so that everyone hears me.

drills.sentence: {{answer}} es spät war, blieben wir noch.
drills.translation: Although it was late, we stayed a while.

drills.sentence: Ich lerne Deutsch, {{answer}} ich in Wien arbeiten will.
drills.translation: I'm learning German because I want to work in Vienna.

drills.sentence: Beeil dich, {{answer}} verpassen wir den Bus!
drills.translation: Hurry up, otherwise we'll miss the bus!

points.title: Verbs with prepositions & da-/wo- words
points.function: Handle fixed verb+preposition pairs and their pronoun forms
points.explanation:
Many verbs pair with a fixed preposition and case: warten auf + acc. 'wait for', sich freuen auf 'look forward to', denken an 'think of'.

When the object is a thing, replace it with a da-compound: darauf, daran; in a question, use a wo-compound: worauf wartest du?

'what are you waiting for?'

drills.translation: We're looking forward to the holidays.
Do you often think of your family?

drills.sentence: Der Bus kommt nicht. Ich warte schon lange {{answer}}.
drills.translation: The bus isn't coming. I've been waiting for it a while.
What are you waiting for? — For the train.

points.title: Adjectival nouns (das Gute, ein Reisender)
points.function: Turn an adjective or participle into a noun
points.explanation:
Adjectives and participles can become nouns, capitalized but still taking adjective endings: der Alte 'the old man', das Gute 'the good (thing)', ein Deutscher 'a German (man)', der Reisende 'the traveller' (from the present participle reisend).

The ending still depends on gender, case, and the preceding article.

drills.sentence: Das {{answer}} an der Sache ist der Preis.
drills.translation: The good thing about it is the price.

drills.sentence: Der {{answer}} sitzt allein auf der Bank.
drills.translation: The old man is sitting alone on the bench.

drills.sentence: Ein {{answer}} fragte mich nach dem Weg.
drills.translation: A traveller asked me for directions.

drills.sentence: Er ist {{answer}} und lebt in Berlin.
drills.translation: He is a German and lives in Berlin.
I wish you all the best!

points.title: Reported speech (Konjunktiv I)
points.function: Report others' words in formal / written German
points.explanation:
Formal German (news, reports) reports speech with Konjunktiv I: er sagt, er sei krank 'he says he is ill', sie meint, sie habe keine Zeit.

Forms come from the infinitive stem + -e/-est/-e/-en…: sei, habe, komme, gehe.

When Konjunktiv I equals the indicative, German switches to Konjunktiv II (er sagt, sie hätten…).

drills.translation: She says she has no time.

drills.sentence: Der Minister erklärte, die Lage {{answer}} ernst.
drills.translation: The minister stated that the situation was serious.
They say he'll come back soon.

drills.sentence: Sie behauptet, sie {{answer}} nichts davon gewusst.
drills.translation: She claims she knew nothing about it.

drills.sentence: Er betonte, es {{answer}} keine andere Lösung.
drills.translation: He stressed that there was no other solution.

points.title: Passive alternatives (man, sich lassen, sein + zu)
points.function: Avoid the werden-passive with lighter constructions
points.explanation: German often replaces the passive: man 'one/you' + active (man baut hier ein Haus 'a house is being built here'); sich lassen 'can be done' (das lässt sich machen 'that can be done'); sein + zu + infinitive 'is to be / can be' (das ist leicht zu verstehen 'that's easy to understand').

drills.translation: The problem can easily be solved.
This text is easy to understand.
There's nothing more to be done.

points.title: Advanced connectors (indem, je … desto, sofern)
points.function: Express means, proportion, and condition precisely
points.explanation: Higher-register connectors: indem 'by …-ing' (er lernt, indem er liest 'he learns by reading'); je … desto/umso 'the more … the more' (je mehr, desto besser); sofern / falls 'provided that / in case'; anstatt … zu 'instead of'; ohne … zu 'without …-ing'.

drills.sentence: Man lernt eine Sprache, {{answer}} man sie spricht.
drills.translation: You learn a language by speaking it.

drills.sentence: {{answer}} mehr man übt, desto besser wird man.
drills.translation: The more you practise, the better you get.

drills.sentence: Je mehr ich lese, {{answer}} mehr verstehe ich.
drills.translation: The more I read, the more I understand.

drills.sentence: Ich helfe dir, {{answer}} du mich rechtzeitig fragst.
drills.translation: I'll help you, provided you ask me in time.

drills.sentence: {{answer}} zu arbeiten, schaute er den ganzen Tag fern.
drills.translation: Instead of working, he watched TV all day.

points.title: Extended attributes & nominal style (Nominalstil)
points.function: Read and build the dense noun phrases of formal German
points.explanation: Formal and academic German packs information into extended attributes — a whole phrase placed between article and noun, often built on a participle: die gestern getroffene Entscheidung 'the decision made yesterday', der in Berlin lebende Künstler 'the artist living in Berlin'. This 'Nominalstil' favours nouns over verbs.

drills.translation: The decision made yesterday was right.

drills.sentence: Der in Berlin {{answer}} Künstler ist berühmt.
drills.translation: The artist living in Berlin is famous.

drills.sentence: Das von allen {{answer}} Buch war ausverkauft.
drills.translation: The book everyone expected was sold out.
The tasks to be solved are difficult.

drills.sentence: Der seit Jahren {{answer}} Konflikt bleibt ungelöst.
drills.translation: The conflict that has lasted for years remains unresolved.

drills.sentence: Die im Bericht {{answer}} Zahlen sind aktuell.
drills.translation: The figures named in the report are current.

points.title: Modal particles (doch, mal, ja, eben)
points.function: Colour a sentence the way native speakers do
points.explanation:
Modal particles carry tone, not literal meaning. doch 'contradiction / encouragement' (komm doch mit! 'do come along!'); mal 'softening' (warte mal 'hold on'); ja 'as you know / surprise' (das ist ja toll!); eben/halt 'resignation' (das ist eben so 'that's just how it is'); wohl 'probably'.

Mastering them is the last step to sounding native.

drills.translation: Do come along, it'll be fun!

drills.sentence: Das ist {{answer}} so, da kann man nichts machen.
drills.translation: That's just how it is, nothing to be done.

drills.sentence: Er ist {{answer}} schon nach Hause gegangen.

drills.translation: Come on, get on with it!

points.title: Stem-changing presents (du fährst, er spricht)
points.function: Conjugate the common vowel-changing verbs
points.explanation:
Many strong verbs change their stem vowel — but ONLY in the du and er/sie/es forms:

e → i: sprechen → du sprichst, er spricht (also essen → isst, geben → gibt, nehmen → nimmt, helfen → hilft).
e → ie: sehen → siehst, sieht; lesen → liest.
a → ä: fahren → fährst, fährt (also schlafen → schläft, tragen → trägt).

All other forms keep the plain stem: wir fahren, ihr fahrt.

related.title: Present tense of regular verbs
related.contrast: Same endings — only the du/er stem vowel shifts: du lernst, but du sprichst.

drills.hint: e → i in the du form

drills.translation: He reads the paper every morning.
drills.hint: e → ie in the er form

drills.translation: Are you driving to Berlin tomorrow?
drills.hint: a → ä in the du form
a → ä in the es form

drills.translation: What do you like to eat for breakfast?
drills.hint: e → i in the du form

drills.translation: We're driving home at the weekend.

points.title: Personal pronouns: accusative & dative (mich, dir)
points.function: Say me, you, him, her as objects
points.explanation:
Pronouns change with case, just like articles.

Accusative (direct object): mich, dich, ihn, sie, es, uns, euch, sie/Sie — Ich sehe dich.
Dative (indirect object, dative verbs, dative prepositions): mir, dir, ihm, ihr, ihm, uns, euch, ihnen/Ihnen — Ich helfe dir. Komm mit mir!

Only ich/du/er change between the two cases for singular — uns and euch pull double duty.

related.title: The dative case (dem, der, den…)
related.contrast: Same case logic as the articles — dem Mann, ihm.

drills.translation: Are you coming to the cinema with us?

points.title: The imperative (Geh! Nehmen Sie!)
points.function: Tell or ask someone to do something
points.explanation:
Three forms, one per way of saying you:

du: the bare stem — Komm! Geh! Mach das! e → i verbs keep the change (Nimm! Hilf! Sprich!), but a → ä verbs LOSE the umlaut (Fahr langsam!).
ihr: the normal ihr form without the pronoun — Kommt! Macht das!
Sie: verb + Sie — Kommen Sie! Nehmen Sie Platz!

sein is irregular: Sei ruhig! Seid leise! Seien Sie so nett!
points.culture_note: Germans soften commands with bitte, mal, or doch: Komm mal her! Machen Sie doch bitte das Fenster auf.

related.title: Stem-changing presents (du fährst, er spricht)
related.contrast: e → i survives in the imperative (Sprich!), a → ä does not (Fahr!).

drills.translation: Please help me with the bag!
